article_title: TEPCO Resumes Commercial Operations at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa Unit 6, the First Post-Fukushima Restart for the Fukushima Operator
summary: Submission approved with a sourcing-diversity concern: all three sources are from Al Jazeera.

content_preview.title: TEPCO Resumes Commercial Operations at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa Unit 6, the First Post-Fukushima Restart for the Fukushima Operator
content_preview.summary: Tokyo Electric Power Company restarted commercial operations at the world's largest nuclear plant on April 16, 2026, ending a 14-year shutdown of TEPCO's entire nuclear fleet since the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi accident.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Tokyo Electric Power Company Holdings (TEPCO) resumed commercial operations of Unit 6 at its Kashiwazaki-Kariwa nuclear power plant on April 16, 2026, ending a shutdown that kept the utility's entire nuclear fleet offline for more than 14 years after the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi accident. The restart marks the first time a TEPCO reactor has returned to commercial service since the triple meltdown that followed the March 2011 Great East Japan Earthquake and tsunami, and it completes a ...

recommendations: Diversify sourcing in future coverage of this story: pair Al Jazeera with a Japanese outlet (NHK, Kyodo, Japan Times) or a wire service (Reuters, AP) to strengthen independent verification.

When the lead event (April 16 commercial restart) is outside the publication window of the cited sources, add a primary-source citation (TEPCO press release or a wire report from the restart day) to close the sourcing gap on the headline claim.

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece at 774 words (within 400-1200 range). Proper sectioning (Overview, What We Know, Context, What We Don't Know). Technical details (control-rod malfunction, 8.2 GW capacity, 15-meter seawall, 15th of 33 operable reactors) are accurately rendered.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 source URLs fetched and read. All are Al Jazeera. (1) https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/12/22/japan-set-to-restart-worlds-biggest-nuclear-power-plant — CONFIRMS: Dec 22, 2025 Niigata Prefectural Assembly consent vote; Governor Hideyo Hanazumi; ~300 protesters with 'No Nukes' and 'Support Fukushima' signs; evacuee Ayako Oga quote 'We know firsthand the risk of a nuclear accident and cannot dismiss it' (verbatim); PM Sanae Takaichi took office two months prior. (2) https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/1/21/japan-to-restart-worlds-biggest-nuclear-plant-after-15-year-shutdown — CONFIRMS: Jan 21, 2026 restart at 7 p.m. local time; 8.2 GW combined output; seven boiling-water reactors; 15th of 33 operable reactors; nearly 40,000 petition signatures; Kobayakawa quote 'an ongoing process, which means operators involved in nuclear power must never be arrogant or overconfident' (verbatim); doubling nuclear to ~20% by 2040; 15-meter tsunami seawall; 2007 earthquake. (3) https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/1/22/japan-shuts-reactor-at-worlds-biggest-nuclear-plant-a-day-after-restart — CONFIRMS: Jan 22, 2026 shutdown a day after restart; control-rod-system electrical malfunction; 'there is no radioactive impact outside' quote (verbatim, attributed by source to spokesperson Takashi Kobayashi; article generalizes to 'a TEPCO spokesperson' — acceptable paraphrase). NOTE: The April 16, 2026 commercial restart date and 4 p.m. local time that form the article's news peg are NOT covered in any of the three cited URLs (all predate April 16). This is a sourcing gap — see concerns.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims traceable to cited Al Jazeera reporting are confirmed verbatim. The 14-year shutdown framing is consistent (March 2011 Fukushima accident to April 2026 restart). The 'first post-Fukushima TEPCO restart' framing is also supported. No hallucinated quotes detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, no sensationalism. Opposition voices and safety concerns are fairly represented alongside TEPCO's and the Takaichi government's perspective.
editor_notes.originality: Distinct from prior March 2026 nuclear pieces: the March 17 Paris tripling-pledge article covers a multilateral coalition announcement, and the March 25 US-Japan SMR piece covers a separate financing deal. This is a Japan-specific operational milestone and does not duplicate earlier coverage. The article appropriately links back to the March 17 tripling-pledge piece for context.
concerns: Sourcing diversity: all 3 sources are from a single outlet (Al Jazeera). Even for a well-reported event, single-outlet sourcing limits independent cross-check of framing and detail.

The headline event — April 16, 2026 commercial-restart commencement at 4 p.m. local time and the ~50-day delay from late February — is not covered by any of the three cited URLs, which all predate April 16. Readers cannot verify the lead news peg from the bibliography.
recommendations: Future Japan nuclear coverage should pair Al Jazeera with a Japanese-language primary outlet in English translation (NHK World, Kyodo News, Japan Times) or a wire service (Reuters, AP) to close single-outlet risk.
For this story specifically, a follow-up could add a TEPCO press release link or a post-April-16 wire report to directly substantiate the commercial-operation start time.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. The article is well-written, factually accurate where sources cover the claims, neutral in tone, and distinct from prior nuclear coverage. Three Al-Jazeera-only sources are a documented diversity concern and the April 16 commercial-restart peg exceeds the publication window of the cited URLs, but the substantive story elements (consent vote, initial restart, malfunction, shutdown) are fully verified against the sources. Concerns are recorded for future improvement rather than blocking publication.
